interface.explanation: Describes the shape an object must have — which properties it needs and their types — without providing any implementation.

type.explanation: Creates a reusable alias for a type, which can be an object shape, a union, a tuple, or anything else.
type.example:
type ID = string | number;
let userId: ID = 42;

enum.explanation: Defines a set of named constant values, useful when a variable should only ever be one of a few known options.
enum.example:
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
const c: Color = Color.Green;

implements.explanation: Declares that a class must provide everything defined by an interface.

extends.explanation: For classes, inherits from a parent class. For interfaces/generics, extends another type or constrains a generic parameter.
extends.example:
interface Animal { name: string; }
interface Dog extends Animal { breed: string; }

public.explanation: Marks a class member as accessible from anywhere (this is the default in TypeScript if you don't specify anything).

private.explanation: Marks a class member as only accessible from inside that same class.

protected.explanation: Marks a class member as accessible from within the class and any of its subclasses, but not from outside.

readonly.explanation: Marks a property that can only be assigned once (usually in the constructor) and can't be changed afterward.

abstract.explanation: Marks a class or method that can't be instantiated directly — it's meant to be extended/implemented by subclasses.

generics.explanation: The <T> syntax lets a function, class, or type work with any type while still keeping type safety, instead of writing separate versions for each type.

as.explanation: A type assertion — tells TypeScript to treat a value as a specific type when you know more about it than the compiler does.

keyof.explanation: Produces a union of all the property names (keys) of a given type, useful for writing generic, type-safe helper functions.
keyof.example:
interface Point { x: number; y: number; }
type PointKey = keyof Point; // 'x' | 'y'

never.explanation: Represents a value that should never occur — typically used for functions that always throw or never return.

unknown.explanation: A safer alternative to 'any' — it can hold any value, but TypeScript forces you to check/narrow its type before using it.

any.explanation: Turns off type checking for a value entirely. Useful as an escape hatch, but it removes the safety TypeScript normally provides.

void.explanation: Used as a return type for functions that don't return a meaningful value.

namespace.explanation: Groups related code (types, functions, classes) under a single named container to avoid naming collisions.

declare.explanation: Tells TypeScript that something exists (defined elsewhere, e.g. in plain JavaScript) without generating any actual code for it.

satisfies.explanation: Checks that a value matches a type, while still letting TypeScript infer the value's own more specific type.
satisfies.example: const config = { mode: 'dark' } satisfies { mode: string };

union type.explanation: The | symbol combines multiple types into one, meaning a value can be any one of them.

non-null assertion.explanation: The ! after an expression tells TypeScript 'trust me, this isn't null or undefined', bypassing its normal null checks.

if.explanation: Runs a block of code only when a condition is true.

for.explanation: A loop that repeats a block of code a set number of times, using a counter variable (start; condition; step).

while.explanation: Repeats a block of code as long as a condition stays true.

function.explanation: Defines a reusable, typed block of code that can take typed inputs and return a typed value.

class.explanation: Defines a typed blueprint for creating objects with shared, type-checked properties and methods.

async.explanation: Marks a function as asynchronous — it returns a Promise and can use 'await' inside it.

await.explanation: Pauses an async function until a Promise settles, giving you its resolved (and typed) value.

try.explanation: Marks a block of code to attempt running, so any error it throws can be caught instead of crashing the program.
